title: Web Application Vulnerability Assessment
description: Learn to identify, exploit, and fix real web application vulnerabilities using OWASP Top 10 methodology — the core skill of every security professional.
learning_outcomes: Identify common web application vulnerabilities by exploring application behaviour
Classify vulnerabilities using OWASP Top 10 and CWE identifiers
Demonstrate exploitation of SQL Injection, XSS, Broken Access Control, CSRF, and Path Traversal
Assess the CIA (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability) impact of each vulnerability
Write corrected code that mitigates each vulnerability type
Apply the OWASP Risk Rating Methodology to prioritise security findings
Produce a structured vulnerability assessment report with evidence
Understand the difference between normal application behaviour and exploited behaviour

ai_tutor_config.personality: Encouraging ethical hacker mentor. Uses real-world breach stories to motivate. Never judges mistakes — treats every wrong answer as a learning opportunity. Celebrates when learners spot vulnerabilities. Reminds learners they're building skills that protect real people.

intervention_triggers.idle_40_seconds: Hey there! Vulnerability hunting takes patience — even professional pentesters stare at screens for a while. Want me to give you a nudge about where to look? 🔍
intervention_triggers.idle_90_seconds: Still thinking? That's totally fine! Security assessment is like detective work — you're looking for clues. Would you like me to explain the concept differently, maybe with a real-world analogy? I can break it down step by step.
intervention_triggers.idle_180_seconds: I know this can feel overwhelming — there's a lot to take in. Let's simplify: focus on just ONE thing. What does the application do with user input? That single question unlocks most web vulnerabilities. Want me to walk through it together?
intervention_triggers.first_error: Not quite — but that's exactly how security professionals learn! Every wrong attempt teaches you something about how the application works. Let me give you a hint about what to look at next.
intervention_triggers.repeated_errors: I can see you're working hard on this — that persistence is exactly what makes a great security analyst. Let me try explaining this from a completely different angle. Think of it like this...
intervention_triggers.after_success: Brilliant work! 🎉 You just found a real vulnerability — the same kind that has caused major data breaches at real companies. That's a genuine security skill. Ready for the next challenge?

explanation_styles.analogy: Web vulnerabilities are like unlocked doors in a building. SQL injection is like a master key — one clever input opens everything. XSS is like leaving a note that every future visitor reads. Broken access control is like a VIP room with no bouncer.
explanation_styles.visual: Follow the data flow: User Input → [BROWSER] → HTTP Request → [SERVER] → Database Query → [DATABASE] → Response → [BROWSER]. Vulnerabilities happen when any step TRUSTS the input without checking it.
explanation_styles.step_by_step: Step 1: Identify where the app takes user input. Step 2: Think about what the app does with that input. Step 3: Ask 'What if I send something unexpected?' Step 4: Try it. Step 5: Observe what happens. Step 6: Classify what you found.
explanation_styles.code_trace: Let's trace the code line by line. The user types into the search box. The servlet reads it with getParameter('q'). Then it puts that DIRECTLY into a SQL string with + concatenation. No PreparedStatement, no escaping. That's the vulnerability — right there on that line.
explanation_styles.comparison: VULNERABLE: String sql = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = '" + input + "'"; vs SAFE: PreparedStatement ps = conn.prepareStatement("SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = ?"); ps.setString(1, input); — Same result, completely different security posture.

lessons.title: Web Application Vulnerability Assessment: From Discovery to Fix

theory.introduction:
Imagine you're a security guard at a massive shopping centre. Your job isn't just to stand at the door — it's to walk every corridor, check every lock, test every fire exit, and find every way a thief could get in BEFORE a real thief does. That's exactly what web application vulnerability assessment is: systematically testing a web application to find security weaknesses before attackers exploit them.

Every major data breach you've heard about — the ones that exposed millions of people's credit cards, passwords, medical records — started with a vulnerability that someone didn't find in time. In 2023, the MOVEit breach affected over 60 million people because of a single SQL injection vulnerability. In 2024, a path traversal flaw in a popular file transfer tool exposed government agencies. These aren't exotic, rare bugs — they're the same vulnerability types you'll learn to find in this module.

The OWASP Top 10 is the global standard that categorises the most critical web application security risks. Think of it as the 'Most Wanted' list for web vulnerabilities. Security professionals worldwide use it to prioritise what to look for. In this module, you'll learn to identify, exploit, and fix the vulnerabilities that appear on this list — using a deliberately vulnerable e-commerce application as your training ground.

This isn't just theory. You'll write real exploit payloads, see real impacts, and write real fixes. By the end, you'll be able to assess any web application's security posture — a skill that's in massive demand across the entire technology industry.
theory.why_important: Web application vulnerabilities are the #1 cause of data breaches worldwide. The average cost of a data breach in 2024 was $4.88 million (IBM Security). Over 90% of web applications contain at least one vulnerability from the OWASP Top 10. Yet most developers never receive formal training in finding or fixing these issues. Learning vulnerability assessment doesn't just make you a better developer — it makes you the person on the team who can prevent the next headline-making breach. Companies actively seek professionals with these skills, and cybersecurity roles are among the fastest-growing and highest-paid in technology. Whether you want to become a penetration tester, a security engineer, or simply a developer who writes secure code, this module builds the foundation you need.

key_concepts.explanation: SQL Injection happens when an application builds database queries by sticking user input directly into a SQL string. It's like giving someone a fill-in-the-blank form, but they write outside the blanks and add their own instructions. Instead of typing a product name like 'headphones', an attacker types something like: ' OR '1'='1 — which changes the SQL logic to return EVERYTHING from the database. This is one of the oldest and most devastating web vulnerabilities, responsible for breaches at Sony, TalkTalk, and Heartland Payment Systems. The fix is simple: use PreparedStatements with parameterised queries, which treat user input as DATA, never as SQL commands.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Think of SQL injection like a restaurant order pad. The waiter writes your order in the blank space. But what if you write 'cancel all orders' in that space? A parameterised query is like a pad where you can ONLY write food names — anything else is ignored.

key_concepts.explanation: XSS happens when an application displays user-provided content without sanitising it, allowing an attacker to inject HTML or JavaScript that runs in OTHER users' browsers. Imagine a product review section: you write a review, but instead of text, you insert <script>alert('hacked')</script>. Every person who views that product page now runs YOUR script. Stored XSS (the type we focus on) is especially dangerous because the malicious payload is saved in the database and shown to every future visitor. A real attacker wouldn't just show an alert — they'd steal session cookies, redirect users to phishing sites, or modify the page content. The fix is output encoding: convert special characters like < > to their HTML entity equivalents (< >) before rendering.
key_concepts.ai_hint: XSS is like someone leaving a booby-trapped note in a library book. You open the book expecting to read, but the note makes your phone call a number automatically. Output encoding is like laminating the pages — you can SEE the note but it can't DO anything.

key_concepts.explanation: Broken Access Control means the application checks whether you're logged in but doesn't check WHETHER YOU'RE ALLOWED to do what you're trying to do. It's the #1 vulnerability in the OWASP Top 10 (2021 and 2025). Imagine an online shop where any logged-in customer can access the admin dashboard just by typing /admin in the URL. The server sees 'yes, this person is authenticated' but never checks 'is this person an admin?' This is called vertical privilege escalation. There's also horizontal access control — where user A can see user B's private data by changing an ID in the URL. The fix requires server-side role checks on EVERY protected endpoint, not just checking if someone is logged in.
key_concepts.ai_hint: It's like a nightclub that checks your ID at the door (authentication) but doesn't check your VIP wristband before letting you into the VIP lounge (authorisation). Two different checks, both essential.

key_concepts.explanation: CSRF tricks a logged-in user's browser into making a request they didn't intend. Here's the scenario: you're logged into your online shop. You visit a different website (maybe clicking a link in an email). That website has a hidden form that automatically submits to YOUR shop, changing your email address or shipping address. Your browser dutifully sends your session cookie with the request — the shop thinks it's you making the change. The attacker never sees your password; they just ride on your existing session. The fix is CSRF tokens: a random, unpredictable value generated per session and included in every form. The server checks the token before processing — since the attacker can't guess the token, their forged request gets rejected.
key_concepts.ai_hint: CSRF is like someone forging a signed cheque. They don't steal your pen — they trick you into signing a blank page, then write the amount themselves. A CSRF token is like writing the amount FIRST in permanent ink before you sign.

key_concepts.explanation: Path Traversal happens when a web application uses user-supplied input to access files on the server without properly validating the path. The attacker uses ../ sequences to 'climb up' out of the intended directory and access any file the server can read. If a download URL looks like /download?file=invoice.pdf, an attacker changes it to /download?file=../../../etc/passwd and suddenly reads system files. This can expose configuration files with database passwords, SSH keys, application source code, and sensitive user data. The fix requires canonicalising the path and verifying it stays within the allowed directory.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Path traversal is like someone asking a librarian for 'the book on shelf 3' but saying 'shelf 3, then go through the Staff Only door, then open the safe.' The librarian should check that all requests stay within the public shelves.

key_concepts.concept: Information Exposure via Error Messages — CWE-209
key_concepts.explanation: When an application crashes, it often shows a detailed error page with stack traces, class names, database schemas, file paths, and server configuration. Developers love this information for debugging — but attackers love it even more. A stack trace reveals the exact technology stack (Java version, server type, database engine), internal class structure (package names, method names), and sometimes even SQL queries with table and column names. This is like leaving the building's blueprints taped to the front door. The fix: show users a generic 'Something went wrong' page and log detailed errors server-side only.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Detailed error pages are like a burglar finding a map of your house with every window and alarm marked. A generic error page is like just showing a 'No Trespassing' sign — it tells them nothing useful.

key_concepts.explanation: Every vulnerability assessment must evaluate impact using the CIA triad: Confidentiality (can an attacker READ data they shouldn't?), Integrity (can they CHANGE data they shouldn't?), and Availability (can they PREVENT legitimate users from accessing the system?). SQL injection typically affects all three. XSS primarily affects Integrity (injecting content) and Confidentiality (stealing cookies/data). Broken Access Control is mainly a Confidentiality issue. CSRF affects Integrity (modifying data without consent). Understanding CIA impact is crucial for prioritising which vulnerabilities to fix first — a vulnerability that affects all three is more critical than one affecting only one.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Think of CIA like protecting a physical safe. Confidentiality = nobody can SEE inside. Integrity = nobody can CHANGE what's inside. Availability = the owner can always OPEN it when they need to.

key_concepts.explanation: The OWASP Top 10 is a standard awareness document for web application security. It represents a broad consensus about the most critical security risks to web applications. The 2025 edition includes: A01:Broken Access Control, A02:Cryptographic Failures, A03:Injection (SQL, command, etc.), A04:Insecure Design, A05:Security Misconfiguration, A06:Vulnerable Components, A07:Authentication Failures, A08:Data Integrity Failures, A09:Logging & Monitoring Failures, A10:Server-Side Request Forgery. When you find a vulnerability, classifying it by OWASP category helps communicate its severity using a language that every security professional understands.
key_concepts.ai_hint: OWASP Top 10 is like a doctor's diagnostic manual. When you find a symptom (vulnerability), you look it up in the manual to give it a proper name and severity level. This way, every security professional speaks the same language.

key_concepts.explanation: A crucial part of vulnerability assessment is understanding the DIFFERENCE between how an application SHOULD behave and how it DOES behave when exploited. A web application with a vulnerability still works correctly for normal users most of the time — that's what makes vulnerabilities hard to spot. For example, a search bar normally returns matching products. Under SQL injection, it returns ALL products including hidden ones. The 'normal' behaviour is correct results; the 'exploited' behaviour is unintended data exposure. When demonstrating a vulnerability, ALWAYS show both: 'here's what it does normally' and 'here's what happens when I send this specific input.' This is how you prove it's a security vulnerability and not just a software bug.
key_concepts.ai_hint: It's like testing a door. Normal: you turn the handle, it opens for people with keys. Exploited: you jiggle it a certain way and it opens for ANYONE. The door works 'fine' for key holders — the exploit is only visible when you try something unexpected.

key_concepts.explanation: Finding a vulnerability is only half the job. The other half is communicating it clearly so developers can fix it. A professional vulnerability report includes: 1) Title (vulnerability name + location), 2) CWE identifier and OWASP category, 3) Severity rating (Critical/High/Medium/Low), 4) Description (what the vulnerability is and where it exists), 5) Steps to reproduce (exact inputs and actions), 6) Evidence (screenshots of normal vs exploited behaviour), 7) Impact (CIA assessment), 8) Recommended fix (specific code-level mitigation). A report that says 'there's a security problem' is useless. A report that says 'SQL injection in /search endpoint, exploitable with this input, impacts confidentiality, fix with PreparedStatement' — that gets fixed immediately.
key_concepts.ai_hint: A vulnerability report is like a doctor's note. 'You're ill' is not helpful. 'You have strep throat, here's the test result, take this antibiotic for 7 days' — now you know exactly what's wrong and how to fix it.

key_concepts.concept: Severity Rating — OWASP Risk Rating Methodology
key_concepts.explanation: Not all vulnerabilities are equal. A SQL injection that exposes millions of records is more critical than an information disclosure that reveals the server version. The OWASP Risk Rating Methodology calculates risk as: Risk = Likelihood × Impact. Likelihood considers: how easy is it to discover? How easy to exploit? How widespread is it? Impact considers: how much data is exposed? Can it be modified? What's the business damage? A Critical vulnerability is easy to find, easy to exploit, and causes major damage (like SQL injection in a login form). A Low vulnerability is hard to find, requires special conditions, and has minimal impact (like exposing a server header).
key_concepts.ai_hint: Think of severity like a weather warning. Critical = tornado touching down (evacuate NOW). High = severe thunderstorm (seek shelter). Medium = heavy rain (bring an umbrella). Low = overcast (you'll be fine, but be aware).

key_concepts.concept: Input Validation vs Output Encoding vs Parameterisation
key_concepts.explanation: Three different defence techniques are often confused. Input Validation checks that data COMING IN matches expected patterns (e.g., an age field only accepts numbers). Output Encoding transforms data GOING OUT so special characters are rendered harmlessly (e.g., < becomes < in HTML). Parameterisation separates CODE from DATA in database queries (PreparedStatements). Each defence protects against different attack types: validation reduces attack surface, encoding prevents XSS, parameterisation prevents SQLi. The mistake is thinking ONE technique covers everything. Defence in depth means using ALL of them at their appropriate points in the data flow.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Think of a post office. Input validation is the size limit (package must fit in the slot). Output encoding is the scanner that deactivates anything dangerous inside. Parameterisation is the labelling system that ensures packages go to the right destination, never to the control room.

complete_examples.title: Example 1: Normal vs SQL Injection — Search Bar
complete_examples.description: Demonstrates how a product search behaves normally vs under SQL injection attack
complete_examples.output:
NORMAL: Search 'headphones'
→ Product: Wireless Headphones — £79.99
(1 result — correct!)

EXPLOITED: Search  ' OR '1'='1
→ Product: Wireless Headphones — £79.99
→ Product: USB-C Hub — £34.99
→ Product: Mechanical Keyboard — £89.99
→ ... (18 more products)
→ Product: INTERNAL: Staff Discount Products — £0.01  ⚠ HIDDEN
→ Product: INTERNAL: Unreleased Prototype X — £999.99  ⚠ HIDDEN
(20 results — hidden products exposed!)
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Line by line:
1. The servlet reads the user's search query from the URL parameter 'q'
2. It builds a SQL string by directly inserting the user's input with + concatenation
3. Normal input 'headphones' creates: WHERE name LIKE '%headphones%' — correct!
4. Malicious input ' OR '1'='1 creates: WHERE name LIKE '%' OR '1'='1%'
5. The OR '1'='1' part is ALWAYS true, so every row matches
6. The hidden=0 check is bypassed because OR changes the query logic
7. Result: ALL products returned including hidden internal ones
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Try modifying the SQL injection to use UNION SELECT to read data from a different table (like users). What would the payload look like?

complete_examples.title: Example 2: Stored XSS — Injecting Script via Review
complete_examples.description: Shows how a product review can contain JavaScript that executes in every visitor's browser
complete_examples.output:
NORMAL REVIEW: "Great headphones, excellent sound quality!"
→ Displays: Great headphones, excellent sound quality!

MALICIOUS REVIEW: <script>alert('XSS — your cookies: '+document.cookie)</script>
→ Displays: [NOTHING visible — but an alert box pops up showing cookies]
→ Every user who views this product page sees the alert
→ A real attacker would steal cookies silently, not show an alert
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
The vulnerability has TWO parts:
1. STORAGE: ReviewServlet saves user content directly to database with no filtering
2. DISPLAY: ProductServlet renders it with printf — browser interprets HTML/JS

The attack chain:
a) Attacker posts review containing <script>...</script>
b) Database stores the script as plain text (database doesn't care what it is)
c) Another user views the product page
d) Server reads review from DB, puts it in HTML response
e) Browser receives HTML with embedded <script> tag
f) Browser executes the script — attacker's code runs in victim's browser

This is 'stored' XSS because the payload persists in the database.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Instead of alert(), write a payload that would redirect the victim to a fake login page to steal their password. How would output encoding prevent this?

complete_examples.title: Example 3: Broken Access Control — Customer Accessing Admin
complete_examples.description: Demonstrates how a normal customer can access admin-only pages by simply changing the URL
complete_examples.output:
NORMAL (as admin): Navigate to /shop/admin/users
→ User: Shop Administrator | Email: admin@shopsecure.local | ...
→ User: Alice Johnson | Email: alice@example.com | Phone: 555-0101 | ...
→ User: Bob Smith | Email: bob@example.com | Phone: 555-0102 | ...
→ (all 6 users visible — correct for admin)

EXPLOITED (as customer1): Type /shop/admin/users in address bar
→ SAME OUTPUT! Customer sees all user data including PII
→ No error, no redirect, no access denied
→ The server only checked 'are you logged in?' not 'are you admin?'
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
The code has a clear logic gap:
Line 5-8: Checks if session exists and userId is set → 'Is this person logged in?'
Line 9-10: Comment shows MISSING role check → 'Is this person an ADMIN?'
Line 12: Goes straight to rendering sensitive data

Authentication ≠ Authorisation:
- Authentication: 'Who are you?' → Session proves identity ✓
- Authorisation: 'What are you allowed to do?' → Role check MISSING ✗

Any customer who knows or guesses the /admin/users URL can access it.
The 'Admin Dashboard' link only shows in the nav for admins, but that's
just UI hiding — the actual server endpoint has no protection.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Fix this servlet by adding proper role checking. Where exactly should the check go? What HTTP status code should be returned for unauthorised access?

complete_examples.title: Example 4: CSRF Attack — Silently Changing User Profile
complete_examples.description: Shows how a malicious webpage can modify a logged-in user's profile without their knowledge
complete_examples.output:
BEFORE ATTACK:
  Name: Alice Johnson | Email: alice@example.com

Attack sequence:
1. Alice is logged into ShopSecure in Tab 1
2. Alice clicks a link that opens evil.html in Tab 2
3. evil.html auto-submits a form to ShopSecure
4. Browser includes Alice's session cookie automatically
5. ShopSecure sees a valid session → processes the update

AFTER ATTACK:
  Name: Account Compromised | Email: hacker@evil.com
  Alice's profile has been silently changed!
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
CSRF exploits the browser's automatic cookie sending:
1. When you log into ShopSecure, your browser stores a session cookie
2. The browser sends this cookie with EVERY request to ShopSecure's domain
3. The attacker's page creates a form targeting ShopSecure's profile endpoint
4. onload='document.forms[0].submit()' means it submits IMMEDIATELY
5. The browser attaches your session cookie to this request
6. ShopSecure sees a valid session + valid form data → updates profile

The server can't distinguish between:
- Alice intentionally updating her profile
- A malicious page submitting the form on Alice's behalf

A CSRF token is the solution: a random value that the attacker can't guess.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Write the FIXED version of doPost that generates and verifies a CSRF token. You'll need both the form (doGet) and the handler (doPost) changes.

complete_examples.title: Example 5: Path Traversal — Reading Server Files
complete_examples.description: Demonstrates how ../  sequences in a file download URL can read arbitrary server files
complete_examples.output:
NORMAL: /shop/download?file=invoice_001.pdf
→ Downloads: Invoice #001 (legitimate invoice PDF)

EXPLOITED: /shop/download?file=../../../etc/passwd
→ Downloads content of /etc/passwd:
   root:x:0:0:root:/root:/bin/bash
   daemon:x:1:1:daemon:/usr/sbin:/usr/sbin/nologin
   student:x:1000:1000::/home/student:/bin/bash
   ...

WORSE: /shop/download?file=../../src/DatabaseHelper.java
→ Downloads application source code with database credentials!
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Path traversal works by abusing relative path resolution:
- 'web/invoices/' is the intended base directory
- '../' means 'go up one directory'
- '../../../' goes up THREE directories from web/invoices/
- This reaches the system root, from where /etc/passwd is accessible

The OS resolves the path:
web/invoices/../../../etc/passwd
  → web/../../../etc/passwd  (up from invoices)
  → ../../etc/passwd         (up from web)
  → ../etc/passwd            (up from project)
  → /etc/passwd              (at system root)

The fix must check that the RESOLVED path stays inside web/invoices/.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Write code that validates the path using Java's Path.normalize() and startsWith() to prevent traversal. What about encoded characters like %2e%2e%2f?

complete_examples.title: Example 6: Fixing SQL Injection with PreparedStatement
complete_examples.description: Complete before/after showing how to convert a vulnerable query to a safe parameterised query
complete_examples.output:
BEFORE FIX — searching ' OR '1'='1 :
→ Returns 20 products (all, including hidden) ❌

AFTER FIX — searching ' OR '1'='1 :
→ Returns 0 products (no product has that literal name) ✅

AFTER FIX — searching headphones:
→ Returns 1 product: Wireless Headphones ✅ (still works normally)
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
The key difference:
BEFORE: sql = "...name LIKE '%" + query + "%'"  →  query becomes part of SQL SYNTAX
AFTER:  sql = "...name LIKE ?"  →  query is injected as a PARAMETER value

With PreparedStatement:
1. The SQL structure is sent to the database FIRST (with ? placeholders)
2. The database COMPILES the query structure
3. Then the parameter values are sent SEPARATELY
4. The database can NEVER confuse data for commands

It's like the difference between:
- Giving someone a sentence with blanks to fill in (PreparedStatement)
- Letting someone write the entire sentence (String concatenation)
complete_examples.ai_challenge: The search needs to match name, description, AND category. Add the additional LIKE clauses to the PreparedStatement. How many ? parameters do you need?

complete_examples.title: Example 7: Fixing XSS with Output Encoding
complete_examples.description: Shows how HTML encoding review content prevents script execution
complete_examples.output:
Review content in DB: BEFORE (vulnerable): Browser receives:

→ Script EXECUTES — alert box appears ❌ AFTER (fixed): Browser receives:

<script>alert('XSS')</script>

→ Displays as visible TEXT: ✅ → No script execution — browser renders entities as text
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
HTML encoding replaces special characters with their entity equivalents:
< becomes <  |  > becomes >  |  & becomes &

Why this works:
- The browser sees <script> in the HTML source
- It knows < is a display character, not a tag delimiter
- So it renders the literal text '<script>' on screen
- No script tag is created, no code executes

IMPORTANT: Encode on OUTPUT, not input. Store the original content
in the database — encode it only when rendering to HTML. This way,
if you need to display the content in a different format (JSON, PDF),
you still have the original data.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What about XSS in HTML attributes? If user input goes into , does HTML encoding alone protect you? Research attribute context encoding.

complete_examples.title: Example 8: Fixing Broken Access Control with Role Checking
complete_examples.description: Complete fix for admin endpoint showing proper authorisation checking
complete_examples.output:
AS ADMIN (admin/shopAdmin123):
Navigate to /shop/admin/users → Full user list displayed ✅

AS CUSTOMER (customer1/custPass1):
Navigate to /shop/admin/users → HTTP 403 Forbidden ✅
"Access Denied: Admin privileges required"

NOT LOGGED IN:
Navigate to /shop/admin/users → Redirected to /shop/login ✅
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
The fix adds a TWO-LAYER check:
Layer 1 (Authentication): Is there a valid session with a userId?
  → No: Redirect to login (you need to prove who you are)
Layer 2 (Authorisation): Does the session have role='admin'?
  → No: Return 403 Forbidden (you're not allowed here)

The isAdmin() method is reusable across all admin endpoints.
This is the Principle of Least Privilege: users should only
access what they NEED for their role, nothing more.

Why 403 (Forbidden) and not 404 (Not Found)?
- 403 tells the user 'this exists but you can't access it'
- Some security teams prefer 404 to avoid revealing page existence
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What about horizontal access control? Customer1 should only see their OWN orders, not customer2's. How would you add that check?

complete_examples.title: Example 9: Fixing CSRF with Token Validation
complete_examples.description: Complete implementation of CSRF token generation, embedding in form, and server-side verification
complete_examples.output:
LEGITIMATE UPDATE (from real form):
→ Form includes csrf_token=a7f3b2c1-...
→ Server compares with session token → MATCH ✅
→ Profile updated successfully

CSRF ATTACK (from evil.html):
→ Attacker's form has no csrf_token field (or a random guess)
→ Server compares: null ≠ a7f3b2c1-... → MISMATCH ❌
→ HTTP 403: CSRF validation failed
→ Profile NOT changed — attack blocked!
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Why this works:
1. UUID.randomUUID() generates a 128-bit random token
   (e.g., 'a7f3b2c1-d4e5-6789-abcd-ef0123456789')
2. This token exists in TWO places: the user's session AND the form HTML
3. When the form submits, both values must match
4. The attacker on evil.com can create a form targeting your server
5. BUT they can't read the CSRF token from your page (Same-Origin Policy)
6. So their forged request arrives without a valid token → rejected

Key: The token is RANDOM and PER-SESSION. Even if an attacker
sees one token, it changes for the next session.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What about single-page applications that use AJAX? They can't use hidden form fields. Research how CSRF protection works with the 'Double Submit Cookie' or 'Custom Header' patterns.

complete_examples.title: Example 10: Fixing Path Traversal with Path Canonicalisation
complete_examples.description: Shows how to validate that file paths stay within the allowed directory
complete_examples.output:
NORMAL: ?file=invoice_001.pdf
  basePath:      /app/web/invoices
  requestedPath: /app/web/invoices/invoice_001.pdf
  startsWith?    YES ✅ → File served

ATTACK: ?file=../../../etc/passwd
  basePath:      /app/web/invoices
  requestedPath: /etc/passwd  (after normalize)
  startsWith?    NO ❌ → 403 Access Denied

SNEAKY: ?file=....//....//etc/passwd
  requestedPath: /etc/passwd  (normalize handles this too)
  startsWith?    NO ❌ → 403 Access Denied
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
The fix uses THREE Java Path operations:

1. toAbsolutePath(): Converts relative path to full path
   'web/invoices' → '/home/student/ShopSecure/build/classes/web/invoices'

2. normalize(): Collapses ../ and ./ sequences
   '/app/web/invoices/../../../etc/passwd' → '/etc/passwd'

3. startsWith(): Checks if resolved path begins with base path
   '/etc/passwd'.startsWith('/app/web/invoices') → false!

This is called 'path canonicalisation' — converting any path
to its simplest, absolute form before checking it.
Without normalize(), an attacker could use encoded characters
or double-dots to bypass simple string checks.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What about URL-encoded path traversal like %2e%2e%2f? Does Java's resolve() handle this? Should you URL-decode the input first or after resolution?

complete_examples.title: Example 11: Information Exposure — Secure Error Handling
complete_examples.description: Replaces detailed stack traces with generic error messages and server-side logging
complete_examples.output:
BEFORE (vulnerable):
  Error: java.sql.SQLException: near "'": syntax error
  at DatabaseHelper.searchProducts(DatabaseHelper.java:142)
  at SearchServlet.doGet(SearchServlet.java:28)
  Server: Jetty 9.4.53 | Java 17.0.2 | Linux 5.15.0

AFTER (fixed):
  Something went wrong
  We've logged this error and will look into it.
  Reference: ERR-1709123456789
  [Return to Home]

Server log (not visible to users):
  [SEVERE] [ERR-1709123456789] java.sql.SQLException: near...
  (full stack trace here for developers)
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
The principle: SEPARATE what users see from what developers see.

Users get: A friendly error page with a reference number they can
report to support. No technical details, no class names, no versions.

Developers get: Full stack trace, request details, and the same
reference number in the server logs for correlation.

What the old error page revealed to attackers:
- Java class names → application architecture
- Line numbers → helps find vulnerable code
- SQL error messages → database schema
- Server version → known vulnerabilities for that version
- OS and Java version → attack surface narrowing
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Some frameworks like Spring have built-in error handling profiles. Research how server.error.include-stacktrace=never works in Spring Boot. How does this compare to our manual approach?

complete_examples.title: Example 12: Complete Vulnerability Report
complete_examples.description: A full professional vulnerability report entry for SQL injection — the format used in real security assessments
complete_examples.code:
// This is not code to run — it's a vulnerability report template.
// Use this format for each vulnerability you find.

/*
=================================================================
VULNERABILITY REPORT: SQL Injection in Product Search
=================================================================

CLASSIFICATION:
  CWE ID:     CWE-89 (SQL Injection)
  OWASP:      A03:2025 — Injection
  Severity:   CRITICAL
  CVSS Score: 9.8 (Critical)

LOCATION:
  Endpoint:   GET /shop/search?q={user_input}
  Source File: SearchServlet.java → calls DatabaseHelper.searchProducts()
  Root Cause:  String concatenation in SQL query construction

DESCRIPTION:
  The product search endpoint passes user input directly into a
  SQL query via string concatenation. This allows an attacker to
  modify the query logic and access unauthorised data.

STEPS TO REPRODUCE:
  1. Navigate to http://localhost:16000/shop/search
  2. In the search box, enter: ' OR '1'='1
  3. Click Search
  4. Observe: All 20 products returned including 2 hidden ones

IMPACT:
  Confidentiality: HIGH — All database records accessible
  Integrity:       HIGH — Data can be modified via injection
  Availability:    MEDIUM — Tables could be dropped

EVIDENCE:
  [Screenshot 1: Normal search for 'headphones' — 1 result]
  [Screenshot 2: Injected search — 20 results with HIDDEN tag]

REMEDIATION:
  Replace Statement with PreparedStatement in
  DatabaseHelper.searchProducts(). Use ? placeholders for all
  user-supplied values.
================================================================
*/
complete_examples.output: This is a report template — no executable output. Use this structure for every vulnerability you document in your assessment.
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
A professional vulnerability report has 8 sections:

1. CLASSIFICATION: CWE + OWASP + Severity → Universal language
2. LOCATION: Exact endpoint + source file → Developer knows WHERE
3. DESCRIPTION: What's wrong in plain English → Anyone can understand
4. STEPS TO REPRODUCE: Exact inputs → Developer can verify
5. IMPACT: CIA assessment → Business understands the risk
6. EVIDENCE: Screenshots → Proof it's real
7. REMEDIATION: Specific fix → Developer knows WHAT to change

Good reports get vulnerabilities fixed FAST.
Vague reports get ignored.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Write a complete vulnerability report for the Broken Access Control issue, following this exact template. Include all 8 sections.

complete_examples.title: Example 13: Union-Based SQL Injection — Extracting User Data
complete_examples.description: Advanced SQL injection technique that reads data from different database tables
complete_examples.output:
Normal search results PLUS:
  ID: 1 | Name: admin | Desc: shopAdmin123 | Price: admin@shopsecure.local
  ID: 2 | Name: customer1 | Desc: custPass1 | Price: alice@example.com
  ID: 3 | Name: customer2 | Desc: custPass2 | Price: bob@example.com
  ...

The user data appears in product columns because UNION
merges the results. Passwords are in the 'description' column,
emails appear as 'prices'. The attacker now has EVERY credential.
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
UNION injection is more powerful than OR injection:

OR injection: ' OR '1'='1 → sees all rows from the SAME table
UNION injection: ' UNION SELECT ... FROM users-- → sees rows from ANY table

The technique:
1. First, find how many columns the original query returns
   (try UNION SELECT null,null,... until no error)
2. Then replace nulls with column names from the target table
3. The -- at the end comments out the rest of the original query

This is why SQL injection is rated CRITICAL:
- It doesn't just expose the searched table
- It can read ANY table in the database
- Including users, passwords, session tokens, admin configs
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What if the application doesn't display error messages (blind SQL injection)? Research how boolean-based and time-based blind SQLi techniques work.

complete_examples.title: Example 14: Defence in Depth — Layered Security
complete_examples.description: Shows how multiple security measures work together to protect an application
complete_examples.output:
Attack attempt: ' OR '1'='1
  Layer 1: Query is 14 chars — passes length check ✅ (not caught)
  Layer 2: PreparedStatement treats it as literal text ✅ BLOCKED
  Result: 0 products found (no product named " ' OR '1'='1 ")

Attack attempt: <script>alert('xss')</script>
  Layer 1: 35 chars — passes length check ✅ (not caught)
  Layer 2: Stored in DB as text (not SQL, so no issue)
  Layer 3: Output encoding renders as visible text ✅ BLOCKED
  Result: Script visible but NOT executable

This is defence in depth — each layer catches what others miss.
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Defence in Depth is like a castle:
- Moat (input validation) — first barrier, stops obvious attacks
- Outer wall (parameterisation) — strong structural defence
- Inner wall (output encoding) — protects even if outer wall breached
- Guards (access control) — verify identity at every door
- Alarm system (error handling) — alerts defenders, hides layout from attackers

No single defence is perfect:
- Input validation can be bypassed with encoding tricks
- PreparedStatements don't protect against XSS
- Output encoding doesn't prevent data theft via SQLi

But COMBINED, they create a security posture where an attacker
must bypass ALL layers to succeed — exponentially harder.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Design a 'security checklist' for a new servlet endpoint. What would you check at each layer? How would you test that each defence works?

visual_diagram.title: Web Application Vulnerability Assessment Workflow
visual_diagram.content:
🔍 Web Application Vulnerability Assessment Workflow
▶ START: Receive Application to Test
│
▼
📋 PHASE 1: RECONNAISSANCE
● Browse the application as a normal user
● Map all pages, forms, and input fields
● Note URL patterns and parameters
● Identify user roles (admin, customer, guest)
● Check what data is visible to each role
│
▼
🎯 PHASE 2: IDENTIFY VULNERABILITIES
Check each input point:

SQLi Does the app put input into database queries? → Try: ' OR '1'='1
XSS Does the app display user content? → Try: <script>alert(1)</script>
BAC Can you access admin pages as customer? → Try: /admin/users in URL
CSRF Do forms have anti-CSRF tokens? → Check: View page source for hidden fields
PATH Does the app serve files by name? → Try: ../../../etc/passwd
INFO Does the app show error details? → Try: Send malformed input
│
▼
💥 PHASE 3: EXPLOIT & DOCUMENT
For each vulnerability found:
✓ Show NORMAL behaviour (screenshot)
✓ Show EXPLOITED behaviour (screenshot)
✓ Record the exact payload/input used
✓ Assess Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability impact
✓ Classify using OWASP Top 10 and CWE
│
▼
🔧 PHASE 4: FIX & VERIFY
SQLi Fix: PreparedStatement with ? parameters
XSS Fix: HTML output encoding (< → <)
BAC Fix: Server-side role check on every endpoint
CSRF Fix: Random token per session, verify on submit
PATH Fix: Path.normalize() + startsWith() check
INFO Fix: Generic error page + server-side logging
│
▼
✅ FINISH: Submit Assessment Report
💡 REMEMBER — The Assessment Checklist:
For each vulnerability:
□ Name and type (e.g., SQL Injection, CWE-89)
□ Location in the app (page, endpoint, input field)
□ CIA impact assessment
□ Severity rating (Critical/High/Medium/Low)
□ Exact exploit payload
□ Screenshot evidence (normal vs exploited)
□ Real-world scenario
□ Fix with code snippet
□ OWASP Top 10 category
🧠 SEVERITY QUICK REFERENCE:
CRITICAL Easy to find + Easy to exploit + Major damage (SQLi in search)
HIGH Easy to find + Requires login + Significant damage (BAC)
MEDIUM Moderate to find + Needs setup + Moderate damage (CSRF, XSS)
LOW Hard to find + Limited damage (Info exposure, server version)

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 1: Spot the Vulnerable Query
practice_exercises.description: Look at the code below. Which line makes this query vulnerable to SQL injection? Identify the problem and explain why it's dangerous.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Let's work on SQL Injection! This exercise focuses on spot the vulnerable query. Take your time — security assessment is detective work. 🔍
practice_exercises.expected_output: Line 2 is vulnerable: the userInput variable is concatenated directly into the SQL string using + operator, allowing an attacker to modify the query logic.

hints.message: Look at how userInput is used in the SQL string. Is it treated as data or as part of the command?
The + operator joins userInput directly into the SQL. What if userInput contained SQL keywords like OR or UNION?
Line 2: String concatenation with user input in SQL = SQL Injection. Fix: use PreparedStatement with ? placeholders.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 2: Fix the SQL Injection
practice_exercises.description: Convert this vulnerable search method to use a PreparedStatement. Replace the string concatenation with parameterised queries.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Let's work on SQL Injection! This exercise focuses on fix the sql injection. Take your time — security assessment is detective work. 🔍

hints.message: Replace Statement with PreparedStatement. Replace the concatenated value with a ? placeholder.
Use conn.prepareStatement(sql) instead of conn.createStatement(). Then use pstmt.setString(1, value) to set the parameter.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 3: Predict the Output — Normal vs Attack
practice_exercises.description:
Given this SQL: SELECT * FROM products WHERE hidden=0 AND name LIKE '%{input}%'

What does the query return when input = headphones? What about input = ' OR '1'='1 ?
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Let's work on SQL Injection! This exercise focuses on predict the output — normal vs attack. Take your time — security assessment is detective work. 🔍
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Scenario A: Returns only products with 'headphones' in the name (probably 1).
Scenario B: Returns ALL products in the database because OR '1'='1' is always true, bypassing the hidden=0 filter. Hidden products are now visible!

hints.message: For Scenario A, think about what LIKE '%headphones%' matches. For Scenario B, trace what happens when the ' character breaks out of the string.
In Scenario B, the injected ' closes the LIKE string early. Then OR '1'='1' adds a condition that's ALWAYS true. What happens to the hidden=0 check?
A: 1 product (Wireless Headphones). B: ALL 20 products including hidden ones. The OR makes '1'='1' always true, and it takes precedence over the AND hidden=0 because of SQL operator precedence.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 4: UNION Injection — Extract User Data
practice_exercises.description: The products table has 8 columns (id, name, description, price, category, stock, hidden, image_url). Write a UNION-based SQL injection payload to extract usernames and passwords from the users table.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Let's work on SQL Injection! This exercise focuses on union injection — extract user data. Take your time — security assessment is detective work. 🔍
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Payload: ' UNION SELECT id, username, password, role, email, full_name, address, phone FROM users--

This returns all product rows PLUS all user rows. User data appears in product columns (username in 'name', password in 'description', etc.).

hints.message: UNION requires the same number of columns in both SELECT statements. The products query has 8 columns. How many columns does your users SELECT need?
Start with: ' UNION SELECT null,null,null,null,null,null,null,null FROM users-- to test. Then replace nulls with actual column names from the users table.

Full payload: ' UNION SELECT id, username, password, role, email, full_name, address, phone FROM users--
The -- comments out the rest of the original query. User credentials appear as 'products' in the results.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 5: Blind SQL Injection Detection
practice_exercises.description: Sometimes the application doesn't show query results directly. Write two boolean-based test payloads: one that should return results (TRUE condition) and one that should return nothing (FALSE condition).
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Let's work on SQL Injection! This exercise focuses on blind sql injection detection. Take your time — security assessment is detective work. 🔍
practice_exercises.expected_output:
TRUE payload: ' OR '1'='1'-- (always true, returns results)
FALSE payload: ' OR '1'='2'-- (always false, returns nothing)

If the app shows different behaviour for these two inputs, SQL injection is confirmed even without seeing error messages.

hints.message: Think of two conditions: one that's always true and one that's always false. How do you write these in SQL?
TRUE: any condition where both sides are equal (1=1, 'a'='a'). FALSE: any condition where they differ (1=2, 'a'='b'). Append these after ' OR ...

TRUE: ' OR '1'='1'--   FALSE: ' OR '1'='2'--
Different results = SQL injection confirmed.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 6: SQLi in Different Contexts
practice_exercises.description: SQL injection doesn't just happen in search boxes. For each scenario below, write the appropriate injection payload.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Let's work on SQL Injection! This exercise focuses on sqli in different contexts. Take your time — security assessment is detective work. 🔍
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. Login: admin'-- (closes username string, comments out password check)
2. Profile: 1 OR 1=1 (no quotes needed for numeric field)
3. Sort: (SELECT version())-- (injects a subquery)

hints.message: For login: how can you make the password check disappear? SQL comments (--) ignore everything after them.
For numeric fields (id), you don't need quotes. Just append OR 1=1. For ORDER BY, you can inject a subquery.

1: admin'--  2: 1 OR 1=1  3: (SELECT version())--
Each context requires slightly different syntax because of where in the SQL the input appears.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 7: Input Validation for SQL — Is It Enough?
practice_exercises.description: A developer 'fixed' SQL injection by blocking the word 'OR' in search input. Find a bypass and explain why this approach is fundamentally flawed.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Let's work on SQL Injection! This exercise focuses on input validation for sql — is it enough?. Take your time — security assessment is detective work. 🔍
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Bypasses:
1. UNION SELECT ... (doesn't contain 'OR')
2. ' AND '1'='1 (uses AND instead of OR)
3. 1' || '1'='1 (uses || concatenation operator)

Blocklisting is fundamentally flawed because you can't anticipate every SQL keyword and syntax variation. PreparedStatements are the only real fix.

hints.message: The filter only blocks 'OR'. What other SQL keywords could achieve similar results? Think about UNION, AND, or operator alternatives.
UNION SELECT doesn't contain 'OR' at all! Also, what about lowercase 'or' vs uppercase 'OR'? Wait — they used toUpperCase(), so case won't help. But UNION still works.
Bypasses: UNION SELECT, AND-based injection, || operator. Blocklisting ALWAYS has gaps. The only real fix is parameterisation (PreparedStatement).

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 8: Multi-Table Data Extraction
practice_exercises.description: Write a complete SQL injection attack plan that extracts: 1) all table names in the database, 2) column names from the users table, 3) all user records. Use SQLite-specific system tables.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Let's work on SQL Injection! This exercise focuses on multi-table data extraction. Take your time — security assessment is detective work. 🔍
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Step 1: ' UNION SELECT type,name,sql,null,null,null,null,null FROM sqlite_master--
(Returns all table names and their CREATE TABLE statements)

Step 2: Read the CREATE TABLE users(...) statement from step 1 results to learn column names.

Step 3: ' UNION SELECT id,username,password,role,email,full_name,address,phone FROM users--

hints.message: SQLite stores all schema info in the sqlite_master table. Its columns include 'name' (table name) and 'sql' (the CREATE TABLE statement).
Step 1 payload: ' UNION SELECT type,name,sql,null,null,null,null,null FROM sqlite_master-- (pad to 8 columns to match products). The 'sql' column shows you exactly what columns each table has.
Full chain: Step 1: ...FROM sqlite_master-- (find tables). Step 2: Read CREATE TABLE from results (find columns). Step 3: ...FROM users-- (extract data). This is systematic database enumeration.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 9: SQLi Impact Assessment
practice_exercises.description: You've confirmed SQL injection in an e-commerce application's search bar. Write a complete CIA impact assessment and calculate the risk severity using the OWASP methodology.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Let's work on SQL Injection! This exercise focuses on sqli impact assessment. Take your time — security assessment is detective work. 🔍
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Confidentiality: HIGH — Attacker can read all tables: user credentials, emails, addresses, order history, payment data, admin configs.

Integrity: HIGH — With INSERT/UPDATE injection, attacker can modify prices, create admin accounts, alter order records.

Availability: MEDIUM — DROP TABLE could destroy data; resource-intensive queries could cause DoS.

Severity: CRITICAL — Likelihood is HIGH (easy to discover via search bar, no authentication needed) × Impact is HIGH (full database access) = CRITICAL.

hints.message: For Confidentiality: think about what's in the database. Users, orders, products, admin data — all accessible via UNION injection.
For Integrity: SQL injection isn't just about reading. INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE are also SQL commands. What damage could they do?

CIA: C=HIGH (all data readable), I=HIGH (data modifiable via UPDATE/INSERT), A=MEDIUM (DROP TABLE possible). Severity: CRITICAL because it's easy to find (public search bar), easy to exploit (simple string), and devastating impact.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 10: Defence Comparison — Which Fix Is Best?
practice_exercises.description: Three developers proposed different 'fixes' for SQL injection. Analyse each one: rate it as Effective, Partially Effective, or Ineffective, and explain why.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Let's work on SQL Injection! This exercise focuses on defence comparison — which fix is best?. Take your time — security assessment is detective work. 🔍
practice_exercises.expected_output:
A: INEFFECTIVE — Blocklist can be bypassed (encoding tricks, double-writing: SESELECTLECT → SELECT after removal, alternative syntax).

B: PARTIALLY EFFECTIVE — Escaping quotes blocks basic injection but can be bypassed with numeric injection, encoding, or database-specific escape sequences.

C: EFFECTIVE — PreparedStatement separates SQL structure from data at the database driver level. No bypass is possible because user input never becomes part of the SQL command.

hints.message: Think about each approach: does it prevent ALL possible SQL injection, or just some patterns?
Developer A: What if someone writes SESELECTLECT? After removing SELECT, it becomes SELECT again. Developer B: What about numeric contexts where quotes aren't needed?

A: Ineffective (bypass: encoding, double-writing, alternative syntax). B: Partially effective (works for string contexts but fragile). C: Effective (PreparedStatement is the gold standard — data and commands are fundamentally separated).

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 11: Identify the XSS Sink
practice_exercises.description: Find where user content is rendered without encoding in this product page code.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Time to tackle XSS! This exercise is about identify the xss sink. XSS is everywhere on the web — let's learn to stop it. 🛡️
practice_exercises.expected_output: Line 12 is the XSS sink. 'username' is properly encoded with htmlEncode(), but 'content' is rendered directly with printf without any encoding. Any HTML or JavaScript in the review content will execute in visitors' browsers.

hints.message: Compare how 'username' and 'content' are handled. Is there a difference in how they're output?
Line 10 uses htmlEncode(username) but line 12 uses content directly. What happens if content contains <script> tags?

Line 12: out.printf("

%s

", content) — no encoding! Fix: out.printf("

%s

", htmlEncode(content));

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 12: Write an XSS Payload
practice_exercises.description: Write three different XSS payloads: 1) a simple alert, 2) a cookie stealer, 3) a page defacement. Explain what each does.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Time to tackle XSS! This exercise is about write an xss payload. XSS is everywhere on the web — let's learn to stop it. 🛡️
practice_exercises.expected_output: 1. PoC: 2. Stealer: 3. Deface:

hints.message: A simple proof-of-concept just needs to execute JavaScript. What's the simplest script tag you can write?
To steal cookies, you need to send document.cookie to an external server. An Image object can make a GET request without visible navigation.
1: 2: 3:

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 13: Build the HTML Encoder
practice_exercises.description: Write a complete htmlEncode method that prevents XSS by converting dangerous characters to HTML entities.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Time to tackle XSS! This exercise is about build the html encoder. XSS is everywhere on the web — let's learn to stop it. 🛡️

hints.message: Start with & → & first. Why? Because if you do < → < first, then & → &, the < becomes &lt; — double encoding!
Chain .replace() calls: input.replace("&", "&").replace("<", "<").replace(">", ">")...
Full solution: return input.replace("&","&").replace("<","<").replace(">",">").replace("\"",""").replace("'","'");

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 14: Reflected vs Stored XSS
practice_exercises.description: Classify each scenario as Reflected XSS or Stored XSS, and explain which is more dangerous and why.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Time to tackle XSS! This exercise is about reflected vs stored xss. XSS is everywhere on the web — let's learn to stop it. 🛡️
practice_exercises.expected_output:
A: REFLECTED XSS — payload comes from the current request (URL/search), only affects the requester, requires victim to click a crafted link.

B: STORED XSS — payload saved in database, affects ALL future visitors automatically, no special link needed.

Stored is more dangerous: it's persistent, affects many users, and requires no social engineering per victim.

hints.message: Reflected means the payload 'bounces' off the server in the response to a single request. Stored means it persists.
Key question: does the payload need to be sent EACH TIME (reflected) or is it saved and served to everyone (stored)?
A: Reflected (one-time, requires crafted link). B: Stored (persistent, affects all visitors). Stored is worse because it scales — one injection, thousands of victims.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 15: XSS Context Matters
practice_exercises.description: XSS payloads differ depending on WHERE in the HTML the injection happens. Write appropriate payloads for each context.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Time to tackle XSS! This exercise is about xss context matters. XSS is everywhere on the web — let's learn to stop it. 🛡️
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. HTML: <script>alert(1)</script>
2. Attribute: " onerror=alert(1) x="  (breaks out of src, adds event handler)
3. JavaScript: ";alert(1)//  (closes string, executes code, comments rest)
4. URL: javascript:alert(1)  (or data:text/html,<script>alert(1)</script>)

hints.message: Each context has different 'escape characters'. In HTML it's < >. In attributes it's ". In JS strings it's " or '. Think about what breaks each context.
For attributes: you need to close the " first, then add an event handler. For JS: close the string " then add ;alert(1).
HTML: <script>alert(1)</script>. Attr: " onerror=alert(1) x=". JS: ";alert(1)//. URL: javascript:alert(1). Each requires breaking out of its specific syntax.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 16: Content Security Policy
practice_exercises.description: A Content Security Policy (CSP) header can prevent XSS even if encoding fails. Write a CSP that blocks inline scripts but allows scripts from your own domain.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Time to tackle XSS! This exercise is about content security policy. XSS is everywhere on the web — let's learn to stop it. 🛡️
practice_exercises.expected_output:
CSP: script-src 'self'; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'; default-src 'self';

'self' = only your own domain. No 'unsafe-inline' for script-src means inline <script> tags won't execute even if injected. This is a powerful second line of defence against XSS.

hints.message: CSP uses directives like script-src and style-src. The value 'self' means 'same origin only'.
script-src 'self' allows /static/js/app.js but blocks <script>alert(1)</script> because inline scripts require 'unsafe-inline'.

CSP: "script-src 'self'; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'; default-src 'self';"
This blocks inline JS (XSS defence), allows your own scripts, allows inline CSS, and defaults everything else to same-origin.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 17: XSS Worm Simulation
practice_exercises.description: In 2005, the Samy worm infected over 1 million MySpace profiles using stored XSS. Design (on paper, not for real!) how a self-propagating XSS worm would work in a product review system.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Time to tackle XSS! This exercise is about xss worm simulation. XSS is everywhere on the web — let's learn to stop it. 🛡️
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Attack chain:
1. TRIGGER: Victim views product with infected review. Script runs automatically.
2. SPREAD: Script uses XMLHttpRequest/fetch to POST a new review containing itself on a random product. The worm now exists on TWO products.
3. IMPACT: Each new viewer triggers steps 1-2 again. Exponential spread. Additionally modifies victim's profile via API call.

Defence: Output encoding stops step 1 entirely. CSP stops script execution. Rate limiting on review posts slows propagation.

hints.message: A worm needs to: execute (XSS), replicate (post itself somewhere new), and optionally cause damage (modify data).
The key to self-replication: the script needs to submit an HTTP request that creates a new review containing the SAME script on a different product.

Chain: 1) Script in review auto-executes. 2) Script calls fetch('/review', {method:'POST', body:...}) posting itself to another product. 3) Exponential spread as each viewer triggers replication. Fix: Output encoding prevents step 1.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 18: DOM-Based XSS
practice_exercises.description: Not all XSS goes through the server. DOM-based XSS happens entirely in the browser. Find the vulnerability in this client-side JavaScript.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Time to tackle XSS! This exercise is about dom-based xss. XSS is everywhere on the web — let's learn to stop it. 🛡️
practice_exercises.starter_code: // Vulnerable line: // Attack URL: // Fix:

hints.message: Every output that contains user-influenced data needs htmlEncode(). Which of the three outputs could contain attacker-controlled content?

hints.message: Think about the difference between checking IF someone is logged in vs checking WHAT they're allowed to do. Spot where authentication ≠ authorisation in this admin servlet.

practice_exercises.ai_intro: CSRF is a sneaky attack — let's learn about identify missing csrf protection. The attacker never sees your password; they just ride on your session. 🎭
practice_exercises.expected_output: See hints for solution to: Identify Missing CSRF Protection

hints.message: CSRF works because browsers automatically send cookies with every request. The server can't tell if a request came from YOUR form or an attacker's form.
The fix is a secret token that the attacker can't guess. Only YOUR form has the correct token; the attacker's forged form doesn't.

For Identify Missing CSRF Protection: Generate with UUID.randomUUID().toString(), store in session, embed in form as hidden field, verify on POST before processing. If tokens don't match → 403 Forbidden.

hints.message: For path traversal: the ../ sequence means 'go up one directory'. Multiple ../../../ sequences can reach the root filesystem.
The fix: resolve the full path, normalize it (collapse ../), then check it starts with the allowed base directory. If not → reject.
For Spot the Path Traversal: Use Path.normalize().startsWith(basePath) for traversal. For info exposure: log.error() server-side, generic message to user, remove Server headers.

practice_exercises.ai_intro: Now let's put it all together! CIA Impact Matrix is a key professional skill. Security analysts write reports like this every day. 📝
practice_exercises.expected_output: See hints for guidance on: CIA Impact Matrix

hints.message: Start with the most important information first. A busy CTO reads the title, severity, and fix — they may not read everything else.
Be specific: don't just say 'there's a security issue.' Say exactly what, where, how to exploit, why it matters, and how to fix it.

For CIA Impact Matrix: Use CWE identifiers for precise classification, OWASP categories for standard communication, and CVSS scores for severity. Include both the exploit evidence AND the remediation code.
